refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall enable end-to-end teleoperation communication with latency that permits safe remote operation by the tele-operator.
refined_fr.rationale: The original FR conflates two distinct obligations: (1) supporting teleoperation capability, and (2) ensuring latency is compatible with safety. The refined version separates the functional intent (enable teleoperation communication) from the performance constraint (latency compatible with safe operation). The FR now resides purely in the problem domain—it specifies what must be achieved (teleoperation with acceptable latency) without prescribing how (network architecture, protocol choice, compression). The acceptance criterion (p95 ≤ 150 ms) is a quantified constraint that flows from this FR and is verified separately.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Measure end-to-end latency (sensor input to actuator command output) under agreed network conditions. Confirm p95 latency ≤ 150 ms. Validate through tele-operator acceptance testing and safety case review that measured latency permits safe remote operation per worksite operational policies.
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-008.1: The NGHE solution shall transmit tele-operator control inputs from remote station to equipment with measurable latency.
FR-NGHE-008.2: The NGHE solution shall transmit equipment state feedback (sensor data, video, telemetry) from equipment to remote station with measurable latency.
FR-NGHE-008.3: The NGHE solution shall ensure cumulative end-to-end teleoperation latency is compatible with safe remote operation under agreed network conditions.

candidate_dps: DP1: Establish a dedicated, low-latency communication pathway with end-to-end latency monitoring and adaptive quality-of-service (QoS) prioritization to ensure operator commands and telemetry reach within the p95 ≤ 150 ms budget.

DP2: Implement a hybrid communication architecture with primary and fallback channels, each independently capable of meeting latency requirements, with automatic failover and latency attestation at each hop.
DP3: Decompose teleoperation into command-response loops with bounded round-trip time guarantees via network reservation, traffic shaping, and deterministic scheduling at each network segment.

DP4: Use edge-local autonomy with operator-in-the-loop validation: minimize latency-critical decisions by delegating safe, bounded actions to local controllers while reserving high-latency decisions for operator confirmation.
DP5: Establish a real-time communication substrate with explicit latency contracts, jitter bounds, and congestion avoidance, decoupled from best-effort data transport.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP3: Decompose teleoperation into command-response loops with bounded round-trip time guarantees via network reservation, traffic shaping, and deterministic scheduling at each network segment.

potential_sources: DP3 couples latency guarantee to network infrastructure provisioning—requires explicit SLA with network operator and assumes network topology is known and controllable.
DP3 assumes command-response loop granularity is compatible with operator cognitive load and safety policies; tight coupling to worksite operational procedures.
DP3 decouples from specific transport protocol (avoids vendor lock-in) but couples to deterministic scheduling capability at each segment—may conflict with shared network environments.
DP3 does not couple to specific sensor/actuator technologies; latency budget allocation across sensing, computation, communication, and actuation remains independent.
coupling_check.notes: DP3 is preferred because it explicitly names the latency constraint as a design driver (bounded round-trip time) and decomposes the problem into independently addressable segments (network reservation, traffic shaping, scheduling). This supports modular IM allocation and testability. DP1 and DP2 are less specific about *how* latency is guaranteed. DP4 introduces operational complexity (edge autonomy) that may conflict with safety policies and is better treated as a complementary mitigation, not the primary DP. DP5 is too abstract and overlaps with DP3.

assumptions_risks: ASSUMPTION: Network path from operator to worksite is provisioned with QoS capabilities (traffic shaping, priority queuing, or reserved bandwidth). RISK: If network is best-effort only, DP3 cannot guarantee p95 ≤ 150 ms. Mitigation: Validate network SLA before system deployment.

ASSUMPTION: Worksite safety plan permits command-response loop latency of up to 150 ms (p95). RISK: If safety policy requires sub-100 ms latency or mandates operator reaction time < 150 ms, DP3 is insufficient. Mitigation: Align DP3 latency budget with safety plan early; consider DP4 (edge autonomy) as complementary.

ASSUMPTION: Teleoperation command-response granularity (e.g., discrete motion commands, sensor polling intervals) is compatible with 150 ms round-trip time. RISK: If operator expects sub-50 ms responsiveness (e.g., continuous joystick control), DP3 may feel sluggish and reduce operator confidence. Mitigation: Define command semantics and operator interaction model explicitly; validate with human factors testing.

ASSUMPTION: Network segments (operator site, ISP backbone, worksite) can be independently instrumented for latency monitoring and scheduling. RISK: If any segment is opaque or uncontrollable (e.g., public internet), latency guarantees degrade. Mitigation: Establish end-to-end latency measurement and fallback to DP2 (hybrid channels) if primary path degrades.

ASSUMPTION: Deterministic scheduling is feasible at each network segment without introducing unacceptable jitter or packet loss. RISK: Scheduling overhead or congestion may cause p95 latency to exceed 150 ms under peak load. Mitigation: Conduct network simulation and load testing; define traffic shaping parameters conservatively.

ASSUMPTION: Operator and worksite are geographically separated such that physical latency (speed of light) is negligible compared to 150 ms budget. RISK: If distance is > 15,000 km, physical latency alone approaches 100 ms. Mitigation: Confirm geographic separation; if extreme distance, revise latency budget or use DP4 (edge autonomy) to reduce operator-in-the-loop frequency.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: FR (refined): The NGHE solution shall enable end-to-end teleoperation communication with latency that permits safe remote operation by the tele-operator, such that command-response round-trip latency is bounded at p95 ≤ 150 ms under agreed network conditions and worksite safety policies. DP3 should be further decomposed into sub-DPs: (1) Reserve network bandwidth and enforce traffic shaping to isolate teleoperation traffic from best-effort flows; (2) Implement deterministic scheduling at each network segment (operator site, backbone, worksite) to bound queuing delay; (3) Establish end-to-end latency monitoring and attestation to detect SLA violations and trigger fallback or operator alert. This decomposition clarifies the architectural boundaries and enables independent IM allocation (network provisioning, scheduling middleware, monitoring service). Additionally, recommend explicit traceability from DP3 to worksite safety plan to confirm that 150 ms latency is operationally acceptable; if not, escalate to DP4 (edge autonomy) as a complementary mitigation.

im_boundary.name: Teleoperation
im_boundary.scope: Encapsulates command-response loop orchestration, network timing guarantees, traffic shaping policies, and deterministic scheduling coordination across network segments to maintain bounded round-trip latency for teleoperated vehicle control.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Receives high-level operator commands and mission directives; Teleoperation module translates these into time-bounded command-response cycles with network reservation parameters.

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Interfaces with network infrastructure to enforce traffic shaping, request bandwidth reservations, and coordinate deterministic scheduling across segments; receives network state feedback for latency monitoring.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Exports round-trip time metrics, command-response cycle status, network reservation state, and scheduling diagnostics for monitoring and adaptation.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Receives safety constraints that bound acceptable round-trip times and command latencies; exports alerts if timing guarantees are at risk or violated.

module_containment_scores.notes: Excellent fit. Command-response loop decomposition, round-trip time guarantees, traffic shaping, and deterministic scheduling are core teleoperation concerns. Network Link interface provides clean boundary for physical network coordination. Minor diffusion: safety constraint validation may require SafetyAlert feedback, but this is well-managed via inout interface.

Poor fit. Vehicle module owns actuator control and state, not network-level timing guarantees or traffic shaping. DP3 is fundamentally about network-layer determinism, not vehicle dynamics. Placing DP3 here would scatter network scheduling logic across vehicle firmware, creating tight coupling to network infrastructure that should be abstracted.

Weak fit. Safety Services owns constraint validation and alert generation, but DP3 is about implementing the mechanisms (reservation, shaping, scheduling) that satisfy safety constraints, not enforcing constraints themselves. Placing DP3 here conflates mechanism with policy; Safety Services should consume Teleoperation's timing guarantees, not own their implementation.

Very poor fit. NGHE Solution is a top-level system container. Placing DP3 here bypasses architectural layering and makes the solution-level module responsible for low-level network scheduling details. High diffusion: DP3 logic would be scattered across multiple subsystems without clear ownership.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: The Teleoperation module is the natural owner of DP3 because command-response loop orchestration with bounded latency is the defining functional concern of teleoperation. Network reservation, traffic shaping, and deterministic scheduling are implementation mechanisms that directly support this core function. The module's interfaces (Tasking in, Network Link inout, Telemetry out, SafetyAlert inout) provide clean boundaries for receiving operator intent, coordinating with network infrastructure, exporting timing diagnostics, and respecting safety constraints.
containment_risks: Risk: Network Link interface may be underspecified. If the actual network infrastructure does not support reservation or traffic shaping APIs, DP3 implementation may require fallback mechanisms or negotiation logic that diffuses into Vehicle or Safety Services.

Risk: Deterministic scheduling at each network segment may require coordination with multiple physical network nodes (routers, switches, gateways). If these nodes are owned by separate modules or external systems, Teleoperation's scheduling logic may become distributed, weakening cohesion.

Risk: Safety constraint validation (SafetyAlert inout) could become bidirectional negotiation if Safety Services rejects proposed round-trip times. This could create circular dependencies if not carefully managed via clear request-response semantics.

Risk: If Vehicle module has its own latency-sensitive control loops (e.g., stability control), those loops may conflict with Teleoperation's command-response timing. Coordination logic may need to diffuse into Vehicle, weakening Teleoperation's containment.
assigned_im.recommended_revision: DP3 is well-chosen and containment in Teleoperation is strong. No revision recommended. However, to further reduce diffusion risk, consider refining DP3 to explicitly state that network reservation and traffic shaping are negotiated via the Network Link interface (not implemented within Teleoperation), and that Teleoperation owns only the command-response loop orchestration and scheduling coordination logic. This clarifies the boundary: Teleoperation decides *what* timing is needed; Network Link provides *how* to achieve it.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP3 directly addresses the FR by decomposing teleoperation into command-response loops with bounded round-trip time guarantees. The mechanisms (network reservation, traffic shaping, deterministic scheduling) are logically sound and necessary to achieve the latency requirement (p95 ≤ 150 ms). DP3 is solution-agnostic enough to permit multiple network technologies while maintaining the core constraint of bounded latency.

diffusion_risks: Network Link interface underspecification: If actual network infrastructure lacks reservation or traffic shaping APIs, DP3 implementation may require fallback negotiation logic that diffuses into Vehicle or Safety Services modules.

Distributed scheduling coordination: Deterministic scheduling across multiple physical network segments (routers, switches, gateways) may require coordination with externally-owned nodes, risking diffusion of scheduling logic beyond Teleoperation module boundaries.

Vehicle control loop conflict: If Vehicle module owns latency-sensitive stability or safety control loops, coordination logic to prevent command-response timing conflicts may diffuse into Vehicle, weakening Teleoperation's cohesion.

Bidirectional safety negotiation: SafetyAlert inout interface could become circular dependency if Safety Services rejects proposed round-trip times, requiring iterative negotiation that blurs request-response semantics.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: Teleoperation module achieves 0.92 containment score with 0.08 diffusion. Command-response loop orchestration, round-trip time guarantees, traffic shaping, and deterministic scheduling coordination are core teleoperation concerns. Module interfaces are well-defined: Tasking (in) for operator intent, Network Link (inout) for infrastructure coordination, Telemetry (out) for diagnostics, SafetyAlert (inout) for constraint feedback. The boundary is clean and cohesive. Minor diffusion stems from external dependencies (network infrastructure capabilities, vehicle control loops, safety policy) rather than internal scattering.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: High. Acceptance criterion (p95 latency ≤ 150 ms) is measurable and traceable to Teleoperation module's Telemetry interface, which exports round-trip time metrics and command-response cycle status. Network Link interface provides feedback on network state and reservation success. Safety constraint validation via SafetyAlert interface ensures worksite safety plan compliance. Verification requires instrumentation of command-response cycles and network monitoring, which is feasible within the module boundary.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Clear. FR allocation to Teleoperation module is explicit and justified. The module owns the mechanisms (reservation, shaping, scheduling) that directly satisfy the latency requirement. Ownership of DP3 is unambiguous. However, allocation clarity depends on explicit specification of what Teleoperation *does not* own: Network Link interface should clarify that Teleoperation negotiates timing requirements but does not implement low-level network device configuration. Vehicle module should clarify that it respects Teleoperation's command-response timing without introducing conflicting control loops.

recommended_revisions: Refine DP3 to explicitly separate concerns: 'Teleoperation module orchestrates command-response loops and coordinates scheduling requirements via Network Link interface; Network Link interface is responsible for translating these requirements into network-level reservation, traffic shaping, and deterministic scheduling policies.' This clarifies the boundary: Teleoperation decides *what* timing is needed; Network Link provides *how* to achieve it.

Specify Network Link interface contract: Define required APIs for network reservation requests, traffic shaping policy negotiation, and scheduling coordination. If actual network infrastructure cannot support these APIs, document fallback mechanisms and their impact on latency guarantees.

Add Vehicle-Teleoperation coordination protocol: Define how Vehicle module's control loops (if any) synchronize with Teleoperation's command-response timing to prevent conflicts. Specify whether Vehicle owns any latency-sensitive feedback loops and how they interact with remote command execution.

Clarify SafetyAlert bidirectionality: Define request-response semantics for safety constraint negotiation. Specify whether Safety Services can reject proposed round-trip times and what happens if rejection occurs (e.g., fallback to degraded mode, operator notification, mission abort).

Document network segment ownership: Identify which network segments (e.g., local network, WAN, gateway) are owned by Teleoperation vs. external systems. Specify coordination mechanisms for segments outside Teleoperation's direct control to mitigate distributed scheduling diffusion risk.

decision.justification: All three gates (FR-DP, DP-IM, FR-IM) pass with high confidence. The FR is well-articulated, DP3 is logically sound and solution-agnostic, and the Teleoperation module boundary is clean with 0.92 containment. However, the gate analysis identifies four critical diffusion risks and five recommended revisions that must be resolved before proceeding to detailed design. Given high safety criticality, the recommended revisions—particularly Network Link interface contract specification, Vehicle-Teleoperation coordination protocol, SafetyAlert bidirectionality semantics, and network segment ownership clarification—are prerequisites for safe decomposition. Rather than stop (which would leave critical interface contracts unspecified) or revise current level (which would delay necessary sub-FR derivation), ZIGZAG_DOWN with explicit sub-FRs that address the interface and coordination concerns identified in the gate analysis. This approach derives the next level while simultaneously resolving the recommended revisions through sub-FR specification.
next_level_sub_frs: FR3.1: Orchestrate command-response loops with bounded round-trip time (p95 ≤ 150 ms) by coordinating operator intent, vehicle state feedback, and network timing constraints.
FR3.2: Negotiate and enforce network-level timing requirements (reservation, traffic shaping, deterministic scheduling) via Network Link interface to guarantee latency bounds.

FR3.3: Coordinate command-response timing with Vehicle module's control loops to prevent conflicts and ensure safe command execution without introducing latency-sensitive feedback loops within Teleoperation.
FR3.4: Validate proposed round-trip times and command execution plans against Safety Services constraints via SafetyAlert interface, with explicit handling of constraint rejection and fallback modes.
FR3.5: Monitor and report round-trip time metrics, command-response cycle status, and network reservation success via Telemetry interface to enable verification and diagnostics.

revise.why: DP3 requires refinement to explicitly separate orchestration concerns from network implementation. Revise DP3 to state: 'Teleoperation module orchestrates command-response loops and coordinates scheduling requirements via Network Link interface; Teleoperation decides *what* timing is needed (round-trip time bounds, command-response cycle constraints); Network Link interface is responsible for translating these requirements into network-level reservation, traffic shaping, and deterministic scheduling policies.' This revision clarifies the boundary and directly supports sub-FR3.2 derivation. Additionally, DP3 should explicitly address Vehicle-Teleoperation coordination (sub-FR3.3) and SafetyAlert bidirectionality (sub-FR3.4) as part of the command-response orchestration logic.

assumptions: Network Link interface can provide APIs for reservation requests, traffic shaping policy negotiation, and scheduling coordination; if not, fallback mechanisms exist and are documented.
Vehicle module either owns no latency-sensitive control loops or has a well-defined synchronization protocol with Teleoperation to prevent timing conflicts.
Safety Services can provide constraint feedback via SafetyAlert interface with clear semantics for acceptance/rejection and fallback behavior.
Network segments (local, WAN, gateway) have identifiable ownership; segments outside Teleoperation's control have documented coordination mechanisms.
Telemetry interface can instrument command-response cycles and network monitoring with sufficient granularity to verify p95 latency ≤ 150 ms acceptance criterion.
open_risks: Network Link interface underspecification: Actual network infrastructure may lack required APIs for reservation or traffic shaping, forcing fallback negotiation logic that diffuses into Vehicle or Safety Services modules. Mitigation: Sub-FR3.2 derivation must specify Network Link contract with explicit fallback mechanisms.

Distributed scheduling coordination: Deterministic scheduling across multiple physical network segments (routers, switches, gateways) may require coordination with externally-owned nodes, risking diffusion of scheduling logic beyond Teleoperation module boundaries. Mitigation: Sub-FR3.2 must clarify network segment ownership and coordination protocols for out-of-scope segments.

Vehicle control loop conflict: If Vehicle module owns latency-sensitive stability or safety control loops, coordination logic to prevent command-response timing conflicts may diffuse into Vehicle, weakening Teleoperation's cohesion. Mitigation: Sub-FR3.3 must define explicit Vehicle-Teleoperation coordination protocol with clear ownership boundaries.

Bidirectional safety negotiation: SafetyAlert inout interface could become circular dependency if Safety Services rejects proposed round-trip times, requiring iterative negotiation that blurs request-response semantics. Mitigation: Sub-FR3.4 must specify SafetyAlert bidirectionality semantics with explicit rejection handling and fallback modes.

High safety criticality amplifies impact of interface underspecification: Any ambiguity in Network Link, Vehicle coordination, or SafetyAlert contracts could lead to unsafe command execution or latency violations. Mitigation: Sub-FR derivation must include explicit interface contracts and safety validation logic.
evidence_needed: Network Link interface specification: Document required APIs for reservation requests, traffic shaping policy negotiation, deterministic scheduling coordination, and fallback mechanisms. Verify that target network infrastructure (e.g., TSN, 5G, proprietary) supports these APIs or has documented fallback behavior.

Vehicle-Teleoperation coordination protocol: Define synchronization semantics between Vehicle module's control loops (if any) and Teleoperation's command-response timing. Specify ownership of latency-sensitive feedback loops and conflict resolution mechanisms.

SafetyAlert interface contract: Define request-response semantics for safety constraint negotiation. Specify whether Safety Services can reject proposed round-trip times, what happens on rejection (e.g., fallback to degraded mode, operator notification, mission abort), and how iterative negotiation is handled.

Network segment ownership map: Identify which network segments (local, WAN, gateway, etc.) are owned by Teleoperation vs. external systems. Document coordination mechanisms for segments outside Teleoperation's direct control.

Telemetry instrumentation specification: Define metrics exported via Telemetry interface (round-trip time distribution, command-response cycle status, network reservation success, latency violations). Verify that instrumentation enables verification of p95 latency ≤ 150 ms acceptance criterion.

Safety validation evidence: Demonstrate that sub-FR3.4 (SafetyAlert coordination) and sub-FR3.3 (Vehicle coordination) prevent unsafe command execution and latency violations under failure modes (network congestion, safety constraint rejection, vehicle control loop conflicts).
